reels.hook: I almost didn't build this. Then my Apple Watch showed me something I couldn't ignore.
reels.script:
I almost didn't build this. Then my Apple Watch showed me something I couldn't ignore.

I was training consistently — or thought I was. Some sessions felt great. Others felt like moving through concrete. Same workouts, same effort, completely different results.

Then I looked at my HRV data in the Health app. Every bad session correlated with a suppressed HRV reading the night before. Every strong session, HRV was elevated.

My body was already telling me. I just had no app translating that signal into an actual decision.

That's the moment Fitovo stopped being an idea and became something I had to build.

If your Apple Watch data could tell you whether today is a push day or a recovery day — would you use it?

Comment READY below. I'll put you on the waitlist directly.

reels.hook: Zone 2 isn't the easy, lazy option. It's where the single most important adaptation in your body happens.
reels.script:
Zone 2 isn't the easy, lazy option. It's where the single most important adaptation in your body happens.

Zone 2 is roughly 60 to 70 percent of your max heart rate — the pace where you can hold a conversation but know you're working.

This is where mitochondrial density is built. Research from Dr Iñigo San Millán links it directly to long-term metabolic health and longevity markers.

But most people training 'moderately hard' are stuck in the grey zone — too hard for Zone 2, not hard enough for Zone 5. Maximum fatigue. Minimum adaptation.

The sweet spot for adults over 40: mostly Zone 2, with occasional Zone 5 sessions — like the Norwegian 4x4 I covered last week.

Your Apple Watch tracks your zone in real time.

Save this — full zone breakdown is in the carousel.
reels.cta: Save this — and follow for the full heart rate zone carousel.

Zone 2 — 60–70% of max HR — is where mitochondrial density is built. Research from Dr Iñigo San Millán links it to some of the strongest longevity markers in exercise science.

After 40, mitochondrial efficiency matters more than ever. Zone 2 isn't the easy option — it's deliberate medicine.

The model that research keeps pointing to:
→ Mostly Zone 2
→ Occasional Zone 5 (Norwegian 4x4)
→ As little time in the middle as possible

reels.hook: Have you ever pushed through a hard workout feeling completely drained — and felt worse for a week afterward?
reels.script:
Have you ever pushed through a hard workout feeling completely drained — and felt worse for a week afterward?

That's not weakness. It's your body signalling something most apps ignore.

Training hard without adequate recovery leads to something called non-functional overreaching — performance drops, recovery extends, hormones shift. You feel like you're going backwards despite putting in the work.

After 40, this happens more. Your recovery window is longer. You cannot bounce back in 24 hours the way you did at 25.

HRV is the clearest readiness signal your body produces. When it's suppressed, training hard is like flooring the accelerator with the handbrake on.

Your Apple Watch measures this while you sleep. Every night. Most people just don't know what to do with the number.

Follow — I'll show you exactly how to use it.
reels.cta: Follow — next video shows you how to actually use your HRV number.
reels.caption:
Pushing through exhaustion doesn't make you tougher after 40.

It makes you slower to recover.

There's a phenomenon called non-functional overreaching — training hard when your body hasn't recovered. Performance drops. Hormones shift. Recovery extends. You feel like you're going backwards even though you're putting in the work.

After 40, recovery windows are longer. That's not an excuse — it's biology.

HRV is the clearest readiness signal your nervous system produces. When it's suppressed, your body is still under load. Training hard on that day is counterproductive.

Your Apple Watch measures this every night while you sleep. For free.

The problem is knowing what to do with the number.

That's exactly what Fitovo is built to solve.

reels.hook: Working out every day doesn't cancel out 8 hours of sitting. The research on this is uncomfortably clear.
reels.script:
Working out every day doesn't cancel out 8 hours of sitting. The research on this is uncomfortably clear.

A landmark study of 800,000 people found prolonged sitting time independently predicted mortality — even in people who exercised regularly. They called it the active couch potato paradox.

After 40, your insulin sensitivity and metabolic health are more sensitive to sedentary time than ever.

The fix isn't more exercise. It's breaking up sitting time. Research suggests two minutes of movement every 30 minutes offsets a significant portion of the harm.

A short walk. A few bodyweight squats. Even just standing up and moving around.

Not a big ask. But a significant difference over decades.

Comment SIT — I'll DM you the full movement snack protocol.
reels.cta: Comment SIT — I'll send you the full movement snack protocol.
reels.caption:
You exercise every day.

And you're still sitting for 8 hours.

A study of 800,000+ people found prolonged sitting predicted mortality independently — even in regularly active people. They named it the 'active couch potato paradox'.

After 40, your insulin sensitivity and metabolic health are more sensitive to sedentary time than they were at 25. Long stretches of sitting compound the problem in ways a single workout can't fully undo.

The prescription isn't more gym time.

It's movement snacks:
→ 2 minutes of movement every 30 minutes
→ A short walk. A few squats. Even just standing.
→ Set a timer if you need to

Small interruption. Significant benefit over decades.

reels.hook: There's a 30-second test researchers link to your decade-long survival odds. Your GP has probably never mentioned it.
reels.script:
There's a 30-second test researchers link to your decade-long survival odds. Your GP has probably never mentioned it.

The sit-to-stand test. Sit cross-legged on the floor and stand back up without using your hands. Each time you use a hand, forearm, or knee for support — minus one point from ten.

A study of 2,000 adults published in the European Journal of Preventive Cardiology found that each point lost was associated with significantly higher all-cause mortality risk.

It's not the sitting and standing that matter. It's what the test reveals: hip mobility, leg strength, balance — everything that determines whether you're functional and independent at 80.

And every single one of those qualities is trainable.

Go try it right now. Then come back and drop your score in the comments.
reels.cta: Try the test — and drop your score in the comments.
reels.caption:
Try this right now.

Sit cross-legged on the floor.
Stand up. Without using your hands.

Each assist — a hand, forearm, or knee — costs you one point from ten.

A study of 2,000+ adults in the European Journal of Preventive Cardiology found that each point lost was significantly associated with all-cause mortality risk over the following decade.

This isn't about the movement itself. It's what the test reveals:
→ Hip mobility
→ Leg strength
→ Balance and coordination
→ Body composition

All the markers of long-term functional independence. All of them trainable.

Drop your score in the comments. Genuinely — I want to know where people are starting from.

And if you want a training plan that actually works on these markers, the waitlist is in the bio. 👆

Consider a simple on-screen scoring graphic: '10 = perfect / -1 per assist'. Avatar should gesture downward when describing the sitting motion, upward for standing. End on warm, inviting CTA — curious and encouraging, not clinical.

carousels.title: The 5 heart rate zones — and why only 2 matter for longevity after 40
slides:
The 5 heart rate zones
— and why only 2 matter for longevity after 40

Zone 1: 50–60% max HR
Active recovery. Easy walks. Feels good.
Adaptation? Minimal.

Zone 2: 60–70% max HR
You can talk in full sentences. Mitochondria are being built.
This is longevity medicine.

Zones 3–4: 70–85% max HR
The 'grey zone'. Feels productive.
Most recreational athletes live here. Research says they shouldn't.

Zone 5: 85–95% max HR
Maximum effort. Norwegian 4x4 territory.
Builds VO₂max. Powerful — in small doses.

The polarised model:
Mostly Zone 2. Occasional Zone 5.
Research consistently shows this outperforms grey-zone training.

How to find your zones
Max HR ≈ 220 minus your age.
Zone 2 = 60–70% of that.
Can you talk in sentences? You're in Zone 2.

Your Apple Watch tracks this in real time.
Fitovo schedules sessions around your zones AND your readiness.
Waitlist → link in bio.
carousels.caption:

carousels.title: The exact 3-workout week for adults over 40 — backed by the research
slides:
The exact 3-workout week for adults over 40
(backed by the research)

Why 3 sessions?

Research shows 3 structured sessions outperform 5 mediocre ones.
Recovery is where adaptation happens.

Session 1: Zone 2 cardio
30–45 minutes. Run, cycle, row.
Conversational pace. Builds mitochondria. Protects your heart.

Session 2: Norwegian 4x4
10 min warm-up → 4 × 4 min at 85–95% HR → cool down.
Total effort: 16 minutes. Builds VO₂max.

Session 3: Strength
3 sets × 6–8 exercises. Squat, hinge, push, pull, carry.
45–60 minutes. Fights sarcopenia.

Recovery matters.

48–72 hours between hard sessions.
Your HRV tells you when you're actually ready.

The rule: stress → recovery → adaptation.

Skip recovery = skip adaptation.
More sessions ≠ more progress after 40.

Fitovo builds this structure for you — automatically.
Adjusted to your readiness every single day.
Waitlist → link in bio.
carousels.caption:
You don't need 5 sessions a week.

You need 3 really good ones — with enough recovery to actually adapt.

Here's the evidence-backed structure for adults over 40. 👉

Save this. Come back to it every Monday.
